healthy.label: Healthy
healthy.message: No Disease Detected. Crop is Healthy.

cercospora_leaf_spot.cause_of_disease: Caused by the fungus Cercospora zeae-maydis, thriving in warm, humid conditions with heavy dew and temperatures between 22–30°C. Spreads through infected crop debris left in the field. Rectangular, grayish-tan lesions run parallel to leaf veins, reducing photosynthesis and yield.
treatment: Apply Propiconazole 25% EC or Azoxystrobin 23% SC fungicide immediately
Remove and destroy infected lower leaves
Improve air circulation by maintaining proper plant spacing
Avoid overhead irrigation to reduce leaf wetness
Rotate crops to reduce fungal buildup in soil

common_rust.cause_of_disease: Caused by Puccinia sorghi, spreading rapidly through airborne spores in cool, moist weather between 16–23°C. Small, circular to elongated powdery brick-red pustules appear on both leaf surfaces. High humidity and dew on leaves accelerate infection.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP or Propiconazole 25% EC immediately at first pustule sighting
Avoid overhead irrigation as it spreads spores
Remove heavily infected leaves to reduce spore load
Use rust-resistant hybrid varieties in next season
Ensure proper field sanitation after harvest

northern_leaf_blight.cause_of_disease: Caused by Exserohilum turcicum, producing large cigar-shaped lesions (2.5–15 cm long) in warm, humid conditions between 18–27°C. Fungal spores overwinter in infected crop debris and spread via wind and rain splashes. Disease starts from lower leaves and moves upward.
treatment: Apply Propiconazole 25% EC or Azoxystrobin + Propiconazole combo immediately
Apply at tasseling to silking stage — this is the most critical treatment window
Remove and burn infected plant debris after harvest
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which promotes disease spread
Use resistant varieties for future planting

healthy.label: Healthy
healthy.message: No Disease Detected. Crop is Healthy.

aphids.cause_of_disease: Caused by Aphis gossypii (cotton aphid), a small soft-bodied insect clustering on leaf undersides and growing tips. Thrives in warm, dry conditions between 20–30°C. Spreads rapidly through winged adults and also transmits viral diseases. Secretes honeydew leading to sooty mold.
treatment: Apply Imidacloprid 17.8% SL or Thiamethoxam 25% WG immediately
Spray during early morning or evening for best absorption
Place yellow sticky traps for monitoring and mass trapping
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which attracts aphids
Rotate insecticide groups to prevent resistance buildup

army_worm.cause_of_disease: Caused by larvae of Spodoptera species. Active at night, hiding in soil during daytime. Thrives in warm, humid conditions. Spreads through adult moth flight and egg-laying. Can devastate entire fields within days if undetected causing complete defoliation.
treatment: Apply Chlorpyrifos 20% EC or Emamectin Benzoate 5% SG immediately
Spray in the evening or early morning when larvae are most active
Remove and destroy egg masses manually during early infestation
Use pheromone traps for monitoring adult moth population
Deep plowing after harvest to expose and kill pupae in soil

medication_timeline.when_to_apply: When larvae are in early instar stage
When larvae are in early instar stage
When larvae are in early instar stage

bacterial_blight.cause_of_disease: Caused by Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. malvacearum, thriving in warm, wet conditions between 25–35°C. Spreads through rain splash, contaminated farm tools, infected seeds, and working in wet fields. Water-soaked angular spots on leaves turning brown/black are the first visible symptoms.
treatment: Apply Copper Oxychloride 50% WP or Streptomycin + Tetracycline combo immediately
Avoid working in the field when it is wet to prevent bacterial spread
Remove and destroy infected leaves and bolls
Use disease-free certified seeds for future planting
Disinfect farm tools regularly with bleach solution

powdery_mildew.cause_of_disease: Caused by Leveillula taurica, favored by warm days, cool nights, and dry conditions between 20–28°C. Spreads through airborne spores. White to grayish powdery growth develops on upper and lower leaf surfaces. Excessive nitrogen fertilization and dense planting promote rapid spread.
treatment: Apply Sulphur 80% WP or Hexaconazole 5% EC immediately
Ensure good air circulation through proper plant spacing
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves
Do not spray Sulphur when temperatures exceed 35°C as it may cause phytotoxicity

medication_timeline.when_to_apply: At first sign of white powdery growth
At first sign of white powdery growth
At first sign of white powdery growth

target_spot.cause_of_disease: Caused by Corynespora cassiicola, thriving in high humidity above 80% and temperatures between 25–32°C. Spreads through airborne spores and rain splash. Circular brown lesions with concentric rings give a target-like appearance. Dense canopy conditions that trap moisture significantly accelerate spread.
treatment: Apply Azoxystrobin 23% SC or Thiophanate Methyl 70% WP immediately
Improve canopy airflow through proper plant spacing
Avoid excessive irrigation especially on foliage
Remove and destroy infected leaves promptly
Apply preventive fungicide sprays during high humidity periods

healthy.label: Healthy
healthy.message: No Disease Detected. Crop is Healthy.

blast.cause_of_disease: Caused by Magnaporthe oryzae, the most destructive rice disease worldwide. Thrives in cool, humid conditions between 20–28°C with heavy dew or rain. Diamond/eye-shaped lesions on leaves (leaf blast) and blackening of neck (neck blast). Excess nitrogen and dense planting worsen the disease.
treatment: Apply Tricyclazole 75% WP or Isoprothiolane 40% EC immediately
Spray at tillering stage for leaf blast control
Spray again at panicle initiation stage for neck blast control
Avoid excess nitrogen fertilization
Drain the field intermittently to reduce humidity

medication_timeline.when_to_apply: At tillering stage and panicle initiation
At tillering stage and panicle initiation
At tillering stage and panicle initiation

brownspot.cause_of_disease: Caused by Bipolaris oryzae, thriving in warm, humid conditions between 25–30°C. Strongly linked to potassium and silicon-deficient soils. Small circular to oval brown spots with a yellow halo appear on leaves, glumes, and grains. Weakened plants due to nutrient stress are most vulnerable.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP or Propiconazole 25% EC immediately
Apply potassium fertilizer to correct soil nutrient deficiency
Treat seeds with Thiram or Carbendazim before sowing as prevention
Remove and destroy infected stubble after harvest
Avoid water stress and maintain proper irrigation

tungro.viral_note: Viral Disease — No Direct Cure. Vector (leafhopper) control is the only management strategy.
tungro.cause_of_disease: Caused by Rice Tungro Bacilliform Virus (RTBV) and Rice Tungro Spherical Virus (RTSV), transmitted by the green leafhopper Nephotettix virescens. Infected plants show yellow-orange leaf discoloration, severely stunted growth, and reduced tillering. Warm, humid conditions and late transplanting increase infection risk.
treatment: Apply Imidacloprid 17.8% SL immediately to control leafhopper vector
Apply Carbofuran 3% G in soil at transplanting as a preventive measure
Remove and destroy all infected plants immediately to prevent spread
Avoid late transplanting which increases leafhopper exposure
Use tungro-resistant rice varieties for future planting

medication_timeline.when_to_apply: At transplanting (preventive) and at first leafhopper detection

healthy.label: Healthy
healthy.message: No Disease Detected. Crop is Healthy.

mosiac.viral_note: Viral Disease — No Direct Cure. Aphid vector control and removal of infected plants is the only management strategy.
mosiac.cause_of_disease: Caused by Sugarcane Mosaic Virus (SCMV), transmitted by aphids especially Melanaphis sacchari. Leaves show alternating light green and dark green mosaic or streaking patterns. Spreads through infected seed cane and aphid movement. Warm, dry conditions with high aphid populations increase infection risk.
treatment: Apply Imidacloprid 17.8% SL or Thiamethoxam 25% WG immediately to control aphid vector
Remove and destroy all infected plant stools immediately
Use only disease-free certified seed cane for planting
Rogue out infected plants promptly to prevent spread to healthy plants
Disinfect cutting tools with bleach solution between plants

medication_timeline.when_to_apply: At first aphid detection or early mosaic symptoms
At first aphid detection or early mosaic symptoms

redrot.cause_of_disease: Caused by Colletotrichum falcatum, the most serious disease of sugarcane. Thrives in warm, humid conditions between 28–32°C. Internal reddening of stalk with white patches and an alcoholic smell. Spreads through infected seed setts, waterlogged soils, and contaminated cutting tools. Most damaging during ratoon crop stage.
treatment: Treat seed setts by soaking in Carbendazim 50% WP solution for 30 minutes before planting
Apply Propiconazole 25% EC or Hexaconazole 5% EC as field spray at early symptoms
Remove and burn all infected clumps immediately
Avoid waterlogging by improving field drainage
Disinfect all cutting tools with bleach before use

rust.cause_of_disease: Caused by Puccinia melanocephala (Brown Rust) or Puccinia kuehnii (Orange Rust). Spreads through airborne spores in warm, humid conditions between 25–30°C. Small, orange-brown pustules appear on underside of leaves. Wind is the primary carrier. Dense planting and excessive irrigation create ideal conditions for rapid spread.
treatment: Apply Propiconazole 25% EC or Triadimefon 25% WP immediately at early pustule stage
Avoid overly dense planting to reduce humidity in the canopy
Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves
Use rust-resistant sugarcane varieties for future planting
Apply preventive fungicide sprays during high humidity season

yellow.viral_note: Viral Disease — No Direct Cure. Aphid vector control and use of certified disease-free planting material is the only management strategy.
yellow.cause_of_disease: Caused by Sugarcane Yellow Leaf Virus (ScYLV), transmitted by the sugarcane aphid Melanaphis sacchari. Yellowing begins on the midrib on the underside of leaves and progresses to full leaf yellowing. Warm, dry weather with high aphid populations accelerates spread. Causes 10–30% yield loss.
treatment: Apply Imidacloprid 17.8% SL or Dimethoate 30% EC immediately to control aphid vector
Use only virus-free certified planting material
Remove and destroy all severely infected plants immediately
Apply balanced potassium fertilization to support plant recovery
Monitor aphid population regularly using yellow sticky traps
